meanings.definition: To deny, nullify, or make ineffective.
examples: One careless mistake can negate hours of hard work.
The new evidence seemed to negate his earlier testimony.

meanings.definition: A person who delivers a formal, eloquent speech in public.
examples: The senator was a gifted orator who captivated crowds.
As an orator, she could hold an audience for hours.

meanings.definition: At an angle of 90 degrees to a given line or surface; exactly upright.
examples: The wall must be perpendicular to the floor.
Draw a line perpendicular to the base of the triangle.

meanings.definition: Excessive boldness or reckless audacity.
examples: He had the temerity to question the judge openly.
She was shocked at his temerity in demanding a raise.

meanings.definition: Playfully fanciful, quaint, or unpredictable.
examples: The garden was full of whimsical statues and fountains.
She has a whimsical sense of humor that delights children.

meanings.definition: An alcoholic, often distilled, intoxicating drink.
examples: The store cannot sell liquor after midnight.
He poured a glass of liquor for his guest.

meanings.definition: Gentle, kindly, and not harmful.
examples: The old teacher had a benign and patient manner.
His criticism was benign and meant to help.

meanings.definition: Not malignant; not cancerous (of a tumor).
examples: The doctor confirmed that the growth was benign.

meanings.definition: Complete agreement among all members of a group.
examples: The proposal passed with rare unanimity.
There was unanimity that the plan should go ahead.

meanings.definition: The quality of being full of energy, excitement, and high spirits.
examples: The children's exuberance filled the playground.
She greeted the news with youthful exuberance.

meanings.definition: Resembling or having the texture or appearance of wood.
examples: The stems become ligneous and stiff as the plant matures.
The fossil had a hard, ligneous surface.

meanings.definition: One of a pair of identical or closely matched things.
examples: The dice came up as a doublet of fives.
The two words are a doublet derived from the same Latin root.

meanings.definition: A close-fitting padded jacket worn by men in Europe from the 14th to 17th centuries.
examples: The actor wore a velvet doublet for the Renaissance scene.

meanings.definition: Relating to or characteristic of the spring season.
examples: The vernal flowers bloomed across the meadow.
Days lengthen after the vernal equinox.

meanings.definition: A brief summary or general overview of a work.
examples: She read a synopsis of the novel before class.
The report opens with a one-page synopsis.

meanings.definition: Short and slightly fat.
examples: The baby reached out with pudgy little hands.
His pudgy fingers struggled with the tiny buttons.

meanings.definition: To enlist or enroll new people, especially for the armed forces or an organization.
examples: The army sought to recruit more volunteers.
The company is trying to recruit talented engineers.

meanings.definition: A newly enlisted or newly joined member.
examples: The new recruit reported for training on Monday.

meanings.definition: Not yet fully grown or developed.
examples: The immature fruit was still hard and green.
The immature birds had not yet grown their adult feathers.

meanings.definition: Behaving in a childish way that lacks emotional maturity.
examples: His immature jokes annoyed the rest of the team.

meanings.definition: Derived from or established by authoritative direction or long-standing custom.
examples: The committee followed the prescriptible rules set by the charter.
Such prescriptible rights had been recognized for generations.

meanings.definition: Highly offensive or disgusting, especially in smell.
examples: A noisome odor rose from the stagnant pond.
The noisome fumes drove everyone from the room.

meanings.definition: Harmful, noxious, or unwholesome.
examples: The swamp was full of noisome vapors.

meanings.definition: Relating to the study of religion, God, and religious belief.
examples: They debated a difficult theological question for hours.
She enrolled in a theological seminary.

meanings.definition: Of only average or moderate quality; not very good.
examples: The film received mediocre reviews from critics.
His mediocre grades surprised his teachers.

meanings.definition: Speaking fluently, readily, and often at great length.
examples: The voluble tour guide kept up a steady stream of facts the whole afternoon.
She became remarkably voluble whenever the topic turned to old films.

meanings.definition: To formally renounce, reject, or repudiate something, often under oath.
examples: The new citizen had to abjure allegiance to any foreign power.
Under pressure, the scholar was forced to abjure his earlier theories.

meanings.definition: The fact or condition of being widespread or common.
examples: Doctors are alarmed by the growing prevalence of diabetes among teenagers.
The survey measured the prevalence of smartphone use in rural areas.

meanings.definition: Dull and tedious because of a lack of variety or change.
examples: The monotonous hum of the engine eventually put her to sleep.
After a week of the same monotonous routine, he longed for a change.

meanings.definition: A state of supreme blessedness or great happiness.
examples: A quiet beatitude settled over the monastery at dawn.
She described her wedding day as a moment of pure beatitude.

meanings.definition: A very large or powerful creature, especially an enormous sea animal.
examples: The whale rose from the depths like an ancient leviathan.
Sailors told tales of a leviathan that could swallow ships whole.

meanings.definition: Something of immense size, power, or influence.
examples: The corporation had grown into a leviathan that dominated the industry.

meanings.definition: Required by law or a rule; obligatory.
examples: Attendance at the morning assembly is compulsory for all students.
Wearing a seatbelt is compulsory in most countries.

meanings.definition: Having the power to affect or shape people, events, or opinions.
examples: She is one of the most influential scientists of her generation.
An influential review can make or break a new restaurant.

meanings.definition: Existing, situated, or operating below the surface of the earth.
examples: The city is honeycombed with subterranean tunnels and passages.
Explorers discovered a vast subterranean lake beneath the mountain.

meanings.definition: Control of a government or public affairs by a disorderly crowd or mob.
examples: Critics warned that the revolution was sliding into mobocracy.
Without the rule of law, the town descended into mobocracy.

meanings.definition: A single example or occurrence of something.
examples: This is just one instance of the company's poor service.
Can you give me an instance of when the rule was actually applied?

meanings.definition: To extend or stretch out, especially the arms or hands.
examples: She outstretched her arms to welcome the children home.
The beggar outstretched a trembling hand for coins.

meanings.definition: Lacking the means to live; very poor and needy.
examples: The clinic provides free care to indigent families.
Many indigent travelers depended on the charity of strangers.

meanings.definition: The practice or state of being married to, or partnered with, only one person at a time.
examples: Many cultures regard monogamy as the basis of marriage.
Certain birds practice monogamy, mating with the same partner for life.

meanings.definition: To murder a prominent person by sudden or secret attack, often for political reasons.
examples: A lone gunman attempted to assassinate the president.
The rebels plotted to assassinate the visiting general.

meanings.definition: Very unusual, remarkable, or beyond what is ordinary.
examples: She showed extraordinary courage during the rescue.
The telescope captured an extraordinary image of the distant galaxy.

meanings.definition: Done openly and unashamedly; conspicuously offensive.
examples: His remark was a blatant lie that fooled no one.
The referee ignored a blatant foul in the penalty area.

meanings.definition: Noisily or offensively loud.
examples: The blatant blare of the loudspeakers drowned out the speaker.

meanings.definition: Able to be seen, noticed, or recognized.
examples: There was no discernible difference between the two samples.
A faint path was just discernible through the fog.

meanings.definition: A petty or trivial objection or criticism.
examples: My only quibble with the plan is its cost.
Setting aside that minor quibble, the report was excellent.

meanings.definition: To argue or raise objections about trivial matters.
examples: They quibbled over who should pay the small tip.

meanings.definition: Free from selfish motive or bias; impartial.
examples: We need a disinterested judge to settle the dispute fairly.
Her advice was valuable because it was entirely disinterested.

meanings.definition: A person who makes or sells eyeglasses and other optical instruments.
examples: The optician fitted her new lenses into the frames.
I took my prescription to the optician to order glasses.

meanings.definition: The act of committing someone or something, such as to prison, a course of action, or the grave.
examples: The judge ordered the committal of the patient to a secure hospital.
The committal of the body took place at a quiet graveside service.

meanings.definition: Dislike of, contempt for, or ingrained prejudice against women.
examples: The film was criticized for its casual misogyny.
Her essay traced the misogyny embedded in old fairy tales.

meanings.definition: A government or system of rule, especially an authoritarian one.
examples: The old regime was overthrown in a bloodless coup.
Citizens chafed under the harsh military regime.

meanings.definition: A particular ordered way of managing or doing something.
examples: She follows a strict training regime before every marathon.

meanings.definition: A formal, often ceremonial, public speech.
examples: The senator delivered a stirring oration on liberty.
His funeral oration moved the entire congregation to tears.

meanings.definition: To burn or sear tissue, especially to stop bleeding or prevent infection.
examples: The surgeon used a hot probe to cauterize the wound.
Field medics had to cauterize the cut to stop the blood loss.

meanings.definition: The amount of food or drink that fills the mouth or is taken in at one time.
examples: She swallowed a mouthful of soup before answering.
He took one mouthful of the cake and asked for more.

meanings.definition: A word or phrase that is long or difficult to say.
examples: That chemical's name is quite a mouthful.

meanings.definition: Conforming to the law, rules, or accepted standards.
examples: She runs a perfectly legitimate import business.
The protesters had a legitimate grievance against the company.

meanings.definition: Justifiable or reasonable.
examples: That is a legitimate concern that we should address.

meanings.definition: The act or process of scattering, dispersing, or gradually disappearing.
examples: The dissipation of the morning mist revealed the valley below.
Engineers studied the dissipation of heat from the device.

meanings.definition: Indulgence in wasteful or self-destructive pleasures.
examples: A life of dissipation ruined both his health and his fortune.

meanings.definition: To pull something up by the roots; to remove or destroy completely.
examples: The storm uprooted dozens of old oak trees.
Reformers sought to uproot corruption from the police force.

meanings.definition: To force someone to leave their home or familiar surroundings.
examples: The war uprooted thousands of families from their villages.

meanings.definition: Extremely angry.
examples: An irate customer demanded to speak with the manager.
She was irate when she found her car had been towed.

meanings.definition: Small beads made from polished shells, strung together and formerly used by some Native American peoples as money and for ornament or record-keeping.
examples: The chief presented a belt of wampum to seal the treaty.
Traders exchanged tools for strings of wampum.

meanings.definition: Without power, strength, or the ability to act.
examples: The villagers felt powerless against the rising floodwaters.
Without evidence, the committee was powerless to act.

meanings.definition: To give in or surrender, often on agreed terms.
examples: After months of siege, the city was forced to capitulate.
The company finally capitulated to the workers' demands.

meanings.definition: A person who entertains by performing tricks and illusions.
examples: The magician pulled a rabbit out of his hat to roaring applause.
At the party, a magician made coins vanish before our eyes.

meanings.definition: A person believed to have magical or supernatural powers; a sorcerer.
examples: In the legend, a powerful magician conjured a storm to defend the castle.

meanings.definition: Moving from one topic to another, often in a rambling way.
examples: His discursive lecture wandered far from the announced subject.
The essay was discursive, touching on history, art, and politics.

meanings.definition: A government department headed by a minister.
examples: The Ministry of Education announced new exam rules.
She works at the foreign ministry as a translator.

meanings.definition: The work or office of a member of the clergy.
examples: He felt called to a ministry serving the poor.

meanings.definition: To voluntarily give up or let go of something.
examples: She refused to relinquish control of the family business.
He had to relinquish his claim to the inheritance.

meanings.definition: Immediately after which; and then.
examples: The bell rang, whereupon the students rushed out of the room.
She finished her speech, whereupon the crowd erupted in applause.

meanings.definition: Too great or extreme to be expressed in words.
examples: She gazed at the sunset with a feeling of ineffable peace.
The beauty of the mountains was almost ineffable.

meanings.definition: Deserving praise; commendable.
examples: Volunteering after the flood was a laudable effort.
Her laudable goal was to give every child access to books.

meanings.definition: Delightful, especially to the taste or other senses.
examples: The bakery filled the street with a delectable aroma.
We enjoyed a delectable meal of fresh seafood.

meanings.definition: A small circle whose center moves around the circumference of a larger circle, used in early astronomy to explain planetary motion.
examples: Ancient astronomers used epicycles to model the wandering of the planets.
Each apparent loop in the planet's path was explained by an epicycle.

meanings.definition: An opinion or conclusion formed on the basis of incomplete information; a guess.
examples: Without more data, his theory remains pure conjecture.
Their account of events was based on conjecture rather than fact.

meanings.definition: To form an opinion or guess without firm evidence.
examples: Historians can only conjecture about the cause of the city's fall.

meanings.definition: The student, usually the highest-ranking, who delivers the farewell address at a graduation ceremony.
examples: As valedictorian, she gave a moving speech at commencement.
He worked hard for four years to become the class valedictorian.

meanings.definition: An object kept as a reminder of a place, person, or event.
examples: She bought a small snow globe as a souvenir of the trip.
He keeps a ticket stub as a souvenir of the concert.

meanings.definition: A position that provides income or status but requires little or no work.
examples: The honorary title was little more than a comfortable sinecure.
Critics accused him of handing out sinecures to his friends.

meanings.definition: A sudden, emphatic cry or remark expressing strong feeling.
examples: She let out an exclamation of surprise when she saw the gift.
His exclamation of delight echoed across the room.

meanings.definition: To prevent something from happening or make it impossible.
examples: A lack of funds precluded any further research.
His injury precluded him from competing in the finals.

meanings.definition: The collective body of nobles in a country, or the rank of a noble.
examples: He was elevated to the peerage for his services to the nation.
Members of the peerage once held seats in the House of Lords.

meanings.definition: To begin a quarrel or attack; to be the first to act with hostility.
examples: Neither side wished to aggress and start an open war.
The smaller nation accused its neighbor of aggressing without provocation.

meanings.definition: First in order of importance; main.
examples: Tourism is the island's principal source of income.
The principal reason for the delay was bad weather.

meanings.definition: To provide with fewer workers or crew than are needed.
examples: The factory was so understaffed that managers had to underman the night shift.
Budget cuts forced the agency to underman its field offices.

meanings.definition: To step over or beyond something.
examples: He had to overstride the puddle to reach the door.
The hiker overstrode the fallen log without breaking pace.

meanings.definition: The process of becoming denser, or of a gas changing into a liquid.
examples: Condensation formed on the cold glass of lemonade.
The water cycle depends on the condensation of vapor into clouds.

meanings.definition: A shortened version of a text or piece of writing.
examples: The magazine printed a condensation of the lengthy report.

meanings.definition: Intended for wide or general circulation.
examples: The bishop issued an encyclical letter to all the parishes.
The decree was encyclical, meant to reach every member of the order.

meanings.definition: To move or whirl around in a circle or spiral.
examples: The dancers gyrated wildly to the pounding music.
The leaf gyrated downward in the swirling wind.

meanings.definition: An officer of a legislative or other assembly responsible for keeping order and enforcing the presiding officer's commands.
examples: The sergeant-at-arms escorted the disruptive visitor from the chamber.
On the speaker's order, the sergeant-at-arms restored quiet to the hall.

meanings.definition: To analyze a sentence by breaking it into its parts and describing the role of each.
examples: Students learned to parse the sentence into subject, verb, and object.
She could parse a Latin sentence quickly and accurately.

meanings.definition: To examine or interpret something closely to make sense of it.
examples: Analysts struggled to parse the meaning of the vague statement.

meanings.definition: Containing or consisting of salt.
examples: The nurse cleaned the wound with a saline solution.
Few plants can survive in such saline soil.

meanings.definition: To express contempt for; to mock or ridicule.
examples: Critics derided the film as shallow and predictable.
They derided his plan as hopelessly impractical.

meanings.definition: Located or placed beneath the tongue.
examples: The medicine comes as a sublingual tablet that dissolves under the tongue.
The dentist examined the sublingual glands for swelling.

meanings.definition: A student or member of a college.
examples: The young collegian spent his evenings studying in the library.
As a collegian, she joined the debate team and the choir.

meanings.definition: The quality of acting suddenly and rashly, without careful thought.
examples: His impetuosity often led him into trouble.
The young captain's impetuosity nearly cost the team the match.

meanings.definition: To put or sink below the surface of water or other liquid.
examples: The submarine began to submerge beneath the waves.
Floodwaters submerged the entire ground floor of the house.

meanings.definition: To completely cover or overwhelm.
examples: She submerged herself in work to forget her worries.

meanings.definition: To settle or snuggle comfortably into a warm, sheltered position.
examples: The kitten nestled against its mother to sleep.
She nestled deeper into the blankets on the cold morning.

meanings.definition: To lie or be situated in a sheltered or partly hidden spot.
examples: The cottage nestled among the pines at the edge of the lake.

meanings.definition: A person who supervises students during an examination.
examples: The proctor walked between the rows to make sure no one cheated.
A proctor collected the test papers when time was up.

meanings.definition: A person appointed to manage the affairs of another; an agent.
examples: He served as proctor for the absent landowner's estate.

meanings.definition: The state of being away or not present.
examples: Her absence from the meeting was immediately noticed.
In the manager's absence, the assistant ran the office.

meanings.definition: The lack or nonexistence of something.
examples: In the absence of any evidence, the case was dismissed.

meanings.definition: To question someone closely or formally, often aggressively.
examples: Detectives interrogated the suspect for several hours.
The committee interrogated the witness about the missing funds.

meanings.definition: To show or suggest that someone is involved in something, especially a crime.
examples: The new evidence implicated two senior officials in the fraud.
He refused to say anything that might implicate his friends.

meanings.definition: Sweets and candy considered collectively, especially as made or sold by a confectioner.
examples: The shop window was filled with brightly colored confectionery.
She had a weakness for chocolate and other confectionery.

meanings.definition: A shop or business where sweets and candy are made or sold.
examples: He owns a small confectionery on the corner of Main Street.

meanings.definition: Causing great suffering, pain, or sorrow.
examples: The soldier suffered a grievous wound in battle.
Losing the championship was a grievous blow to the team.

meanings.definition: Very serious or severe in nature.
examples: He was charged with a grievous offense against the state.

meanings.definition: A poem or song expressing sorrow, especially for someone who has died.
examples: The poet wrote a moving elegy for his late friend.
Her elegy captured the grief of an entire nation.

meanings.definition: Able to withstand or not be affected by something.
examples: This fabric is resistant to water and stains.
The new strain of bacteria is resistant to most antibiotics.

meanings.definition: Opposing or unwilling to accept something.
examples: The committee was resistant to any change in policy.

meanings.definition: To limit the amount of something that each person is allowed to have, especially when supplies are scarce.
examples: During the war, the government rationed sugar and butter.
They had to ration their water carefully on the long hike.

meanings.definition: A fixed amount of a commodity officially allowed to each person during a time of shortage.
examples: Each family received a weekly ration of bread.

meanings.definition: Able to be repaired or put right.
examples: The damage to the engine was minor and easily reparable.
Fortunately the harm to the relationship proved reparable.

meanings.definition: Resembling or moving like waves.
examples: The undulous hills rolled gently toward the horizon.
Her undulous hair fell in soft waves over her shoulders.

meanings.definition: An orchestral piece played at the beginning of an opera, oratorio, or ballet.
examples: The orchestra opened the evening with a stirring overture.
Everyone fell silent as the overture began.

meanings.definition: An approach or proposal made to someone with the aim of starting discussions or a relationship.
examples: The company made friendly overtures toward its rival.

meanings.definition: A very short moment of time.
examples: For an instant, she thought she saw a ghost.
The lightning vanished in an instant.

meanings.definition: Happening or produced immediately.
examples: The movie was an instant success.
Just add water for an instant meal.

meanings.definition: A person who owns or holds land.
examples: The wealthy landholder controlled thousands of acres.
Each landholder was required to pay a tax on the property.

meanings.definition: A line of verse consisting of ten syllables.
examples: Much of English heroic poetry is written in the decasyllable.
The teacher asked us to compose a single decasyllable.

meanings.definition: The way a person behaves and carries themselves.
examples: Her dignified deportment impressed the interview panel.
The school emphasized good manners and proper deportment.

meanings.definition: To honor and remember an important person or event.
examples: The monument commemorates the soldiers who died in the war.
They held a ceremony to commemorate the founding of the city.

meanings.definition: To harm the reputation or credibility of someone or something.
examples: The rumors were spread to discredit the candidate.
New evidence discredited the old theory.

meanings.definition: To cause something to be disbelieved or doubted.
examples: The lawyer tried to discredit the witness's testimony.

meanings.definition: So ordinary or commonplace as to be boring and unoriginal.
examples: The movie's plot was utterly banal and predictable.
They made banal small talk about the weather.

meanings.definition: Able to be transferred to the ownership of another, especially with regard to land or property.
examples: The estate was held as alienable property and could be sold.
Some rights are alienable, while others cannot be given away.

meanings.definition: Capable of being made perfect or improved.
examples: He believed that human nature was perfectible through education.
The design is good but still perfectible with a few tweaks.

meanings.definition: Weakened or worn out, especially by old age or long use.
examples: The decrepit old man could barely climb the stairs.
They lived in a decrepit house with a leaking roof.

meanings.definition: A speech or piece of writing that warmly praises someone or something.
examples: The retiring professor received a glowing encomium from his colleagues.
Critics heaped encomiums on the young author's first novel.

meanings.definition: A minor illness or physical complaint.
examples: The doctor treated a variety of common ailments.
She suffered from a persistent ailment that left her tired.

meanings.definition: Easily angered; bad-tempered.
examples: The choleric old colonel shouted at anyone who disturbed him.
His choleric temper made him difficult to work with.

meanings.definition: A person who examines something in detail using systematic methods.
examples: The financial analyst predicted a rise in stock prices.
As a data analyst, she spends her days interpreting statistics.

meanings.definition: Difficult to understand; obscure.
examples: The lecture on abstruse mathematical theory lost most of the audience.
His writing is full of abstruse references that few readers grasp.

meanings.definition: Guided by one's sense of right and wrong; careful and diligent.
examples: She is a conscientious student who never misses a deadline.
A conscientious worker, he double-checked every detail.

meanings.definition: A cool room, especially one used for keeping food or, in ancient Roman baths, for cold bathing.
examples: The meat was stored in the frigidarium to keep it fresh.
After the hot baths, the Romans would plunge into the frigidarium.

meanings.definition: To complete something or make it better by adding what is needed.
examples: A glass of red wine complements the rich meat dish.
Her calm nature complements his energetic personality.

meanings.definition: Acceptance of and conformity to traditional or established beliefs, especially in religion.
examples: He questioned the orthodoxy of his church's teachings.
Economic orthodoxy held that the budget must always be balanced.

meanings.definition: Able to be brought into harmony or made compatible.
examples: Their differing views proved reconcilable after a long talk.
The two accounts of the event are not easily reconcilable.

meanings.definition: To drive out an evil spirit from a person or place by religious or ritual means.
examples: The priest was called to exorcise the haunted house.
They believed a ritual could exorcise the demon from the child.

meanings.definition: To get rid of a troubling memory or feeling.
examples: She wrote the memoir to exorcise the ghosts of her past.

meanings.definition: A hillside or steep bank, especially in Scotland.
examples: The sheep grazed on the grassy brae above the village.
They climbed the brae to get a better view of the loch.

meanings.definition: The act of devoting something wholly to a particular purpose or cause.
examples: The dedication of the new library drew a large crowd.
The church held a service for the dedication of the altar.

meanings.definition: Wholehearted commitment and hard work toward a goal.
examples: Her dedication to her students was admired by everyone.

meanings.definition: Not occurring or found often; rare.
examples: Snow is uncommon in this part of the country.
It is not uncommon for cats to sleep most of the day.

meanings.definition: Remarkable or unusual in a positive way.
examples: She showed uncommon courage during the crisis.

meanings.definition: Following the latest fashion; stylish.
examples: She arrived in a modish coat that turned every head.
The caf√© had a modish, minimalist interior.

meanings.definition: Moving from one place to another, especially seasonally or in search of work.
examples: Migrant workers harvest the fruit each summer.
The reserve protects many species of migrant birds.

meanings.definition: A person or animal that moves from one region to another.
examples: The migrants traveled hundreds of miles to find work.

meanings.definition: Lying directly beneath or below something.
examples: The fossils were found in the subjacent rock layer.
Erosion exposed the subjacent strata of the cliff.

meanings.definition: Intense feeling of passion, enthusiasm, or devotion.
examples: She spoke of her cause with great ardor.
His youthful ardor for adventure never faded.

meanings.definition: To lower someone in rank, dignity, or standing.
examples: The officer was degraded for disobeying orders.
Such treatment degrades the people it is meant to help.

meanings.definition: To break down or reduce in quality or condition.
examples: Pollution can degrade the quality of the water over time.

meanings.definition: A braced framework of timber or metal used to support a bridge or other horizontal structure.
examples: The train crossed the river on a tall wooden trestle.
Engineers inspected the trestle for signs of rust.

meanings.definition: A supporting frame with legs, used to hold up a table top or workbench.
examples: They set the boards across two trestles to make a table.

meanings.definition: To conduct or carry on business or other dealings.
examples: The partners met weekly to transact the firm's business.
They transacted the sale entirely online.

meanings.definition: Noisy and difficult to control.
examples: The obstreperous crowd refused to quiet down.
Their obstreperous toddler shouted throughout the meal.

meanings.definition: To know or expect that something will happen before it does.
examples: No one could foresee the sudden collapse of the market.
She foresaw the problems that the new plan would cause.

meanings.definition: Made of or coming from oak wood.
examples: They gathered around the heavy oaken table.
The wine aged in oaken barrels for two years.

meanings.definition: To avoid telling the truth by speaking in an evasive or misleading way.
examples: When asked where he had been, he began to prevaricate.
Stop prevaricating and give me a straight answer.

meanings.definition: Sticking or clinging firmly to a surface or thing.
examples: The adherent mud clung to the bottom of his boots.
A thin, adherent film coated the surface of the metal.

meanings.definition: A person who supports a particular party, leader, or set of beliefs.
examples: The new religion quickly gained thousands of adherents.

meanings.definition: Saying little; reserved and not inclined to talk.
examples: Her taciturn grandfather rarely spoke at dinner.
He was a taciturn man who let his actions speak for him.

meanings.definition: To confuse or mix something up so it becomes unclear.
examples: Don't muddle the instructions or you'll lose your way.
Too much information just muddled her thinking.

meanings.definition: A confused or disordered state.
examples: The accounts were left in a complete muddle.

meanings.definition: A claim made by a defendant against the plaintiff in response to the original lawsuit.
examples: The company filed a counter-claim accusing the supplier of fraud.
His lawsuit was met with a counter-claim for damages.

meanings.definition: A woman admired as the most beautiful or charming in a particular place or gathering.
examples: She was the belle of the ball that evening.
Once the belle of the town, she had many admirers.

meanings.definition: A military force made up of ordinary citizens rather than professional soldiers, raised in an emergency.
examples: The local militia gathered to defend the town.
Each able-bodied man was expected to serve in the militia.

meanings.definition: To admit or acknowledge that something is true, often reluctantly.
examples: She conceded that he had a point.
The senator conceded the election after the votes were counted.

meanings.definition: To surrender or give up something.
examples: The army was forced to concede the territory to the enemy.

meanings.definition: A painful, difficult, or prolonged experience that severely tests a person.
examples: Surviving the shipwreck was a terrible ordeal.
The long trial was an ordeal for the whole family.

meanings.definition: An open area or courtyard in front of a building, opening toward the street.
examples: Cars were parked in the forecourt of the hotel.
A fountain stood in the forecourt of the palace.

meanings.definition: To suggest or indicate something without stating it directly.
examples: Her tone implied that she disapproved of the plan.
Are you implying that I made a mistake?

meanings.definition: To involve something as a necessary consequence.
examples: Rights imply responsibilities.

meanings.definition: To make something continue or last indefinitely, preserving it from being forgotten.
examples: The festival perpetuates an ancient local tradition.
Such stereotypes only perpetuate prejudice.

meanings.definition: A person who manages or directs the affairs of an organization, business, or institution.
examples: The hospital administrator approved the new budget.
As a system administrator, she keeps the network running.

meanings.definition: One's family or ethnic descent; the line of people from whom one is descended.
examples: She traced her ancestry back to the early settlers.
He took pride in his Scottish ancestry.

meanings.definition: Excessively talkative, especially about trivial matters.
examples: The garrulous old man told the same stories again and again.
Her garrulous neighbor could chat for hours about nothing.

meanings.definition: The galaxy that contains our solar system, seen in the night sky as a faint band of light.
examples: On a clear night you can see the Milky Way stretching overhead.
Our sun is just one of billions of stars in the Milky Way.

meanings.definition: A net drawn along the bottom of a body of water or across the ground to catch fish or game.
examples: The fishermen hauled in their dragnet full of fish.
A dragnet was pulled across the pond to clear the weeds.

meanings.definition: A coordinated effort by police to catch criminals or suspects.
examples: Police set up a dragnet to capture the escaped prisoner.

meanings.definition: A proposed explanation made as a starting point for further investigation.
examples: The scientists tested their hypothesis with a series of experiments.
Her hypothesis was that sleep improves memory.

meanings.definition: Money in the form of coins rather than paper notes.
examples: The merchant demanded payment in specie, not banknotes.
The bank kept a large reserve of specie in its vault.

meanings.definition: Located on or near the sea.
examples: The maritime towns thrived on fishing and trade.
Canada has several maritime provinces along the Atlantic.

meanings.definition: Relating to the sea, ships, or shipping.
examples: Maritime law governs disputes that occur at sea.

meanings.definition: To greatly enjoy or take pleasure in something.
examples: She relished every bite of the delicious meal.
He did not relish the thought of telling them the bad news.

meanings.definition: A spicy or savory condiment served with food.
examples: He spread sweet relish on his hot dog.

meanings.definition: To place two or more things side by side, often to highlight a contrast.
examples: The artist juxtaposed bright colors against a dark background.
The film juxtaposes scenes of wealth and poverty.

meanings.definition: To heat something beyond its normal or expected temperature, especially a liquid above its boiling point without it boiling.
examples: The microwave can superheat water until it suddenly erupts.
Engineers superheat the steam to drive the turbine more efficiently.

meanings.definition: A small bundle or package of things.
examples: She tied the letters into a neat packet.
He handed me a packet of seeds for the garden.

meanings.definition: A unit of data transmitted over a network.
examples: The message was broken into packets and sent across the internet.

meanings.definition: To agree with someone or share an opinion.
examples: The experts all concur that the climate is changing.
I concur with your assessment of the situation.

meanings.definition: To happen together or coincide.
examples: Several factors concurred to cause the accident.

meanings.definition: To drink something quickly and greedily.
examples: He guzzled a whole bottle of soda in one go.
The thirsty runners guzzled water at the finish line.

meanings.definition: To provide someone with a quality, ability, or gift.
examples: Nature had endued her with great patience.
The training endued the recruits with new confidence.

meanings.definition: The quality of being useful or practical.
examples: The new tool proved its utility on the very first day.
They questioned the utility of holding another meeting.

meanings.definition: A public service such as water, gas, or electricity.
examples: The rent includes all utilities except internet.

meanings.definition: To write or enter a name or words in a book, list, or document.
examples: The clerk inscribed each new member's name in the official register.
Her achievements were inscribed on the honor roll.

meanings.definition: To carve, engrave, or mark words onto a surface such as stone or metal.
examples: The jeweler inscribed their initials inside the ring.

meanings.definition: Belief in or acceptance of something as true.
examples: The detective gave little credence to the witness's wild story.
New evidence lent credence to her theory.

meanings.definition: Having a physical, material body rather than being spiritual or abstract.
examples: The ghost longed to return to corporeal form.
Philosophers debated whether the soul could exist apart from corporeal matter.

meanings.definition: The quality of having a sharp, strong smell or taste.
examples: The pungency of the chili made his eyes water.
She loved the pungency of fresh garlic in the sauce.

meanings.definition: Sharpness or biting force in expression or feeling.
examples: The pungency of his criticism stung the audience.

meanings.definition: The quality of being open, honest, and frank in speech.
examples: She answered every question with refreshing candor.
His candor about his mistakes earned everyone's respect.

meanings.definition: An object kept as a reminder of a person or event.
examples: She kept the seashell as a memento of their summer trip.
He gave her a photograph as a memento of their friendship.

meanings.definition: A weight hung so it can swing freely, used to regulate the movement of a clock.
examples: The old grandfather clock's pendulum swung steadily back and forth.
He watched the pendulum mark each passing second.

meanings.definition: The state of being next to or near something else.
examples: The adjacency of the two buildings made it easy to move between them.
Their offices' adjacency allowed for constant collaboration.

meanings.definition: An unwillingness to put up with delay or to wait calmly.
examples: He tapped his foot with growing impatience.
Her impatience showed as the meeting dragged on.

meanings.definition: A small flaw or mark that spoils the appearance or perfection of something.
examples: A single blemish marred the otherwise flawless apple.
The polished table had not a blemish on its surface.

meanings.definition: To praise highly in words or song.
examples: Critics lauded the young author's first novel.
The choir lauded the heroes with a triumphant hymn.

meanings.definition: The leading figure or main champion in a contest, cause, or enterprise.
examples: She became the chief protagonist of the reform movement.
He was the protagonist behind the company's bold new venture.

meanings.definition: The main character in a story, novel, play, or film.
examples: The protagonist of the novel struggles to find her place in the world.

meanings.definition: Relating to the land or region over which a state or person has control.
examples: The two nations argued over territorial boundaries.
The treaty resolved a long-standing territorial dispute.

meanings.definition: Defensive of an area regarded as one's own.
examples: The dog became territorial whenever strangers approached the yard.

meanings.definition: A person who helps another commit a crime or wrongdoing.
examples: The thief and his accomplice fled in a waiting car.
She was charged as an accomplice to the robbery.

meanings.definition: A building or office where a business keeps its accounts and conducts financial affairs.
examples: The merchant spent long hours in the counting-house balancing his ledgers.
Clerks bustled about the counting-house recording every transaction.

meanings.definition: Relating to the physical body rather than the mind or spirit.
examples: Corporal punishment is banned in most schools today.
The illness caused him great corporal suffering.

meanings.definition: To arrange or structure something again in a more effective way.
examples: The company decided to reorganize its departments to cut costs.
She reorganized the files so everything was easier to find.

meanings.definition: To form opinions or guesses about something without firm evidence.
examples: Reporters began to speculate about the cause of the delay.
We can only speculate as to what he was thinking.

meanings.definition: To invest in something risky in the hope of large gains.
examples: He speculated in stocks and lost much of his savings.

meanings.definition: A group of women united by a shared bond, cause, or religious vow.
examples: The nuns formed a devoted sisterhood within the convent.
A strong sisterhood grew among the women on the team.

meanings.definition: A person who has the right to vote in an election, especially one represented by an official.
examples: The senator listened carefully to the concerns of her constituents.
He promised to serve every constituent in his district.

meanings.definition: A part or component of a whole.
examples: Hydrogen is a constituent of water.

meanings.definition: A person who has abandoned a belief held to be true for one regarded as false.
examples: The bishop denounced him as a pervert from the faith.
To the orthodox, the reformer was nothing but a pervert.

meanings.definition: An instrument that measures atmospheric pressure, used in forecasting weather.
examples: The falling barometer warned the sailors of an approaching storm.
She tapped the barometer to check whether rain was likely.

meanings.definition: Anything that indicates changes or trends in something.
examples: Consumer spending is often a barometer of economic health.

meanings.definition: Happening or moving with great speed.
examples: The patient made a rapid recovery.
Technology is changing at a rapid pace.

meanings.definition: Brave, courageous, and noble in spirit.
examples: The gallant soldier returned to rescue his fallen comrade.
She made a gallant effort despite the odds.

meanings.definition: Politely attentive and chivalrous, especially toward women.
examples: He made a gallant bow and offered her his arm.

meanings.definition: The part of a scene or picture that appears nearest to the viewer.
examples: A small boat sat in the foreground of the painting.
Mountains rose behind the meadow in the foreground.

meanings.definition: To set someone or something free from confinement or bondage.
examples: The troops liberated the prisoners from the camp.
The new law liberated thousands from forced labor.

meanings.definition: Low in pitch or tone.
examples: He sang the part in a deep bass voice.
The bass notes of the organ filled the cathedral.

meanings.definition: A small grain or particle.
examples: She stirred a few granules of sugar into her coffee.
The fertilizer came in tiny green granules.

meanings.definition: The act of leaving out or refusing to admit something or someone.
examples: His exclusion from the team left him deeply disappointed.
The policy resulted in the exclusion of certain groups.

meanings.definition: A means of approaching or entering a place.
examples: The only access to the cabin was a narrow dirt road.
Wheelchair access is provided at the rear entrance.

meanings.definition: The right or opportunity to use or obtain something.
examples: Students were given access to the online library.

meanings.definition: A violent crash in which two or more things strike together.
examples: The two cars were wrecked in a head-on collision.
A collision between the ships was narrowly avoided.

meanings.definition: A clash between opposing ideas, interests, or forces.
examples: The debate revealed a collision of values.

meanings.definition: A trusted person to whom one confides private matters and secrets.
examples: Her sister was her closest confidant.
The king relied on a single confidant for honest advice.

meanings.definition: To make something impure by adding inferior or harmful ingredients.
examples: Dishonest sellers adulterated the milk with water.
The wine had been adulterated with cheap additives.

meanings.definition: A punishment or penalty imposed for breaking a law or rule.
examples: The penalty for speeding is a heavy fine.
He paid the penalty for his carelessness.

meanings.definition: A disadvantage imposed on a team or player for breaking the rules of a game.
examples: The referee awarded a penalty after the foul.

meanings.definition: A decrease in the value or worth of something over time.
examples: The car suffered rapid depreciation in its first year.
Currency depreciation made imports more expensive.

meanings.definition: Sullen, gloomy, and ill-tempered.
examples: He sat alone in a morose silence.
The rainy weather left her feeling morose.

meanings.definition: To decide or determine something in advance, especially by fate or divine will.
examples: He believed his destiny had been preordained.
Some thought the outcome was preordained from the start.

meanings.definition: A particular performance or interpretation of something, such as a song or role.
examples: Her rendition of the song brought the audience to tears.
The orchestra gave a stirring rendition of the symphony.

meanings.definition: A translation or version of a text.
examples: This is a modern rendition of the ancient poem.

meanings.definition: A shortened version of a book, play, or other work.
examples: She read an abridgment of the lengthy novel.
The abridgment kept the main plot but cut minor scenes.

meanings.definition: The act of undermining or overthrowing something from its foundations, especially an established system or authority.
examples: The regime accused them of subversion.
The novel was banned for its subversion of traditional values.

meanings.definition: A person who claims to be able to foretell the future.
examples: The soothsayer warned the king of coming danger.
Villagers visited the soothsayer to learn their fortunes.

meanings.definition: A false or misleading image or impression presented to the senses or mind.
examples: The mirage was an optical illusion caused by the heat.
She clung to the illusion that things would soon improve.

meanings.definition: The earliest known original inhabitants of a country or region.
examples: The aborigines had lived on the land for thousands of years.
Scholars studied the customs of the region's aborigines.

meanings.definition: Able to be divided or separated into parts.
examples: The estate was partible among the surviving heirs.
Under that law, land was partible between sons.

meanings.definition: A title of honor given to certain high-ranking officials such as ambassadors or governors.
examples: His Excellency the ambassador addressed the assembly.
The letter was sent to Her Excellency the governor.

meanings.definition: Absolutely necessary or required; that cannot be avoided.
examples: It is imperative that we leave before the storm hits.
Speed was imperative if the rescue was to succeed.

meanings.definition: Place or stead; used chiefly in the phrase 'in lieu of,' meaning instead of.
examples: She took extra vacation days in lieu of a bonus.
He sent flowers in lieu of attending the ceremony.

meanings.definition: To give greater dignity, honor, or nobility to someone or something.
examples: Acts of kindness ennoble the human spirit.
The king ennobled the loyal knight for his bravery.

meanings.definition: The ability or qualification to do something effectively.
examples: She demonstrated great competence in handling the crisis.
The job requires a high level of technical competence.

meanings.definition: The distance from the top or surface of something to its bottom.
examples: The divers measured the depth of the lake.
Plant the seeds at a depth of two inches.

meanings.definition: The quality of being intellectually or emotionally profound.
examples: Her essay showed remarkable depth of thought.

meanings.definition: The number below the line in a fraction, showing how many equal parts the whole is divided into.
examples: In the fraction three-quarters, four is the denominator.
To add the fractions, give them a common denominator.

meanings.definition: Lacking self-confidence; shy and unassertive.
examples: He was too diffident to ask for a raise.
Her diffident manner hid a sharp and capable mind.

meanings.definition: A feeling of nervous fear or uneasiness about something that may happen.
examples: She opened the exam results with trepidation.
He approached the dark cave with great trepidation.

meanings.definition: A raw material or product that can be bought and sold.
examples: Oil is one of the world's most valuable commodities.
Wheat is traded as a commodity on global markets.

meanings.definition: Just begun and not yet fully formed or developed.
examples: His plans were still inchoate and full of gaps.
The movement was inchoate, lacking clear leadership.

meanings.definition: A person or thing that refers someone or something to another.
examples: The referrer earns a small reward for each new customer.
Her doctor was the referrer who sent her to the specialist.

meanings.definition: A homeless, neglected person, especially an abandoned child.
examples: The orphanage took in waifs from the city streets.
She looked like a frightened waif in the storm.

meanings.definition: To remember or call something back to mind.
examples: I cannot recollect where I left my keys.
She tried to recollect the events of that night.

meanings.definition: A hopeful attitude that good will prevail and things will turn out well.
examples: Despite the setbacks, she faced the future with optimism.
His optimism kept the team's spirits high.

meanings.definition: The philosophical view that the world and its course are ordered for the best.
examples: The philosopher's optimism held that this is the best of all possible worlds.

meanings.definition: To become or make abnormally thin, especially through illness or starvation.
examples: The long famine emaciated the villagers.
Disease had emaciated the once-strong man.

meanings.definition: To hand over a person accused of a crime to the authorities of another country or state.
examples: The government agreed to extradite the fugitive.
He fled abroad to avoid being extradited.

meanings.definition: A small flock of birds, especially quails or partridges.
examples: A covey of quail burst from the underbrush.
The hunters startled a covey of partridges.

meanings.definition: An inscription on a tomb or monument in memory of the person buried there.
examples: The epitaph on the headstone read 'Beloved Father.'
She composed a moving epitaph for her grandmother.

meanings.definition: Having plenty of room; spacious.
examples: The capacious trunk held all their luggage.
They moved into a capacious new house.

meanings.definition: A complete loss of hope.
examples: A wave of despair swept over the stranded sailors.
In despair, she gave up the search.

meanings.definition: A curve traced by a point on the rim of a circle as it rolls around the outside of another circle.
examples: The students plotted an epicycloid in their geometry lesson.
The gear's teeth were shaped along an epicycloid.

meanings.definition: A gesture or expression of greeting or welcome, by word or act.
examples: He raised his hat in salutation as she passed.
The letter began with a warm salutation.

meanings.definition: The act of lying hidden in order to make a surprise attack.
examples: The soldiers fell into an ambush along the mountain road.
They set an ambush for the approaching convoy.

meanings.definition: A military operation in which forces surround a place to cut it off and force its surrender.
examples: The city endured a siege that lasted for months.
The army laid siege to the fortress.

meanings.definition: A large store or marketplace offering a wide variety of goods.
examples: The emporium sold everything from spices to silk.
Tourists wandered through the bustling emporium.

meanings.definition: In spite of that; notwithstanding.
examples: The hike was exhausting; nevertheless, we reached the summit before noon.
She was nervous but spoke clearly nevertheless.

meanings.definition: One who entices or lures another into wrongdoing or evil.
examples: The con artist played the tempter, dangling easy riches before his victims.
In the story, a sly tempter persuades the hero to break his vow.

meanings.definition: Painful, agonizing, or exhausting labor or effort.
examples: After years of travail, the inventor finally perfected his design.
The pioneers endured great travail crossing the mountains.

meanings.definition: The labor and pain of childbirth.
examples: She was in travail through the long night before her child was born.

meanings.definition: To call on someone or something, such as a deity or authority, for help, support, or protection.
examples: The priest invoked the gods to bless the harvest.
She invoked the memory of her mentor for courage.

meanings.definition: To cite or appeal to a law, rule, or principle as a reason or justification.
examples: The lawyer invoked an old statute to defend her client.

meanings.definition: To bring about or call forth a feeling, response, or result.
examples: The painting invokes a deep sense of nostalgia.

meanings.definition: Able to be reached, entered, or approached easily.
examples: The hidden cabin was accessible only by a steep mountain trail.
They added a ramp to make the building accessible to wheelchair users.

meanings.definition: Easy to understand, obtain, or interact with.
examples: The author made complex science accessible to ordinary readers.

meanings.definition: The outer border or edge of a region, especially of a town or city.
examples: They bought a quiet house on the outskirt of the city.
Factories were built on the outskirts, away from the residential center.

meanings.definition: Not certain or fixed; done as a trial or experiment and subject to change.
examples: We made a tentative plan to meet on Friday, pending confirmation.
She gave a tentative answer, unsure of the exact figures.

meanings.definition: Hesitant or unsure in action or manner.
examples: He took a few tentative steps onto the frozen pond.

meanings.definition: The state of depending on or trusting in someone or something.
examples: Their reliance on imported fuel left the country vulnerable.
Too much reliance on calculators can weaken mental arithmetic.

meanings.definition: A flat, portable case for carrying loose papers, drawings, or documents.
examples: The artist carried her sketches in a leather portfolio.
He slipped the contract into his portfolio before the meeting.

meanings.definition: A collection of samples of a person's work, used to show their skills.
examples: She updated her portfolio before applying for design jobs.

meanings.definition: A range of investments held by a person or organization.
examples: He diversified his portfolio to reduce financial risk.

meanings.definition: A person, animal, or plant descended from a particular ancestor.
examples: She is a direct descendant of one of the original settlers.
Modern birds are descendants of certain dinosaurs.

meanings.definition: Extremely large in size, amount, or degree.
examples: An enormous oak tree shaded the entire yard.
The project required an enormous amount of effort.

meanings.definition: Being something in essence or effect, though not formally or in name.
examples: The deputy was the virtual ruler while the king was abroad.
Her silence was a virtual admission of guilt.

meanings.definition: Existing or carried out by means of computers or the internet rather than physically.
examples: The team held a virtual meeting across three time zones.
Students explored the museum through a virtual tour.

meanings.definition: The tongue or a tongue-like part, especially in anatomy or zoology.
examples: The textbook labeled the lingua in the diagram of the insect's mouth.
The frog extends its lingua to catch passing flies.

meanings.definition: To make something lawful by official action.
examples: Several states moved to legalize the sale of fireworks.
The new government legalized political parties that had been banned.

meanings.definition: An authoritative written instruction, especially one from a doctor authorizing a medicine or treatment.
examples: The doctor wrote a prescription for antibiotics.
You need a prescription to buy this medication.

meanings.definition: A recommendation or rule that is authoritatively put forward.
examples: The report offered a prescription for reforming the school system.

meanings.definition: Showing great care, attention, and persistent effort.
examples: Her assiduous study paid off when she topped the exam.
The assiduous gardener tended every plant daily.

meanings.definition: A person appointed to govern a state because the monarch is a minor, absent, or incapacitated.
examples: The queen's brother served as regent until the young king came of age.
During the king's illness, a regent ruled in his name.

meanings.definition: To break apart or burst, especially by force or strain.
examples: The explosion ruptured the fuel tank.
A blood vessel ruptured, requiring emergency surgery.

meanings.definition: To break or sever a relationship or agreement.
examples: The dispute ruptured the long alliance between the two nations.

meanings.definition: The fact or state of being or existing.
examples: Scientists debate the existence of life on other planets.
The old custom has been in existence for centuries.

meanings.definition: A particular way or condition of living.
examples: They led a quiet existence in the remote village.

meanings.definition: A small room that leads into a larger one, often used as a waiting area.
examples: Guests waited in the anteroom before being shown into the hall.
A narrow anteroom opened onto the grand ballroom.
